ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for banana prata; mapped to the closest parent class 'flatbread food product' as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible category for prata/roti canai; no banana-filled variant code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for banana prata or roti prata as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of banana prata is approximately 180 grams, equivalent to one piece of banana butter prata as commonly sold at Singapore hawker centres and prata shops.

nutrient_source.title: Banana Prata — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (Paratha, whole wheat, cooked with ghee) for saturated fat estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, sodium_mg, dietary_fibre_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, magnesium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 — exact match for banana prata. Saturated fat (3.5g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for paratha cooked with ghee/butter (USDA FDC ID 2346413, 'Paratha, whole wheat, cooked'), which reports approximately 3.4–3.6g saturated fat per 100g for a comparable fried wheat flatbread with butter/ghee; this is used as the closest approved-source analogue since the HPB Foodo entry does not report saturated fat separately. Trans fat and vitamin C are set to -1 as these values are not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Roti (Indian flatbread, wheat flour) GI entry; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entry for chapati/roti.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for banana prata in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain roti/chapati (wheat flour flatbread, GI approximately 62), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database. The banana filling and pan-frying in butter or ghee are expected to moderately influence the glycaemic response; however, the high dietary fibre content (10.2g/100g) partially attenuates postprandial glucose elevation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (62) × net carbohydrate per serving (75.23g/100g × 180g serving = 135.4g) / 100 = 83.9, rounded to 84.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632mg/100g exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold; a single 180g serving delivers approximately 1,138mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2,000mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit banana prata to occasional consumption, request reduced salt in dough preparation, and avoid honey or condensed milk toppings that add empty-calorie sugars contributing to insulin-driven sodium retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total carbohydrate is high at 75.2g/100g, but dietary fibre at 10.2g/100g substantially attenuates the glycaemic response; estimated GI of 62 (medium) and a glycaemic load of approximately 84 per 180g serving indicate significant postprandial glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to half a piece (approximately 90g), avoid honey or sugar toppings, and pair with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose rise; the high fibre content is a mitigating factor but does not eliminate the carbohydrate burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 3.5g/100g from butter and ghee raises LDL cholesterol risk; sodium at 632mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit banana prata to occasional consumption, request preparation with vegetable oil instead of butter or ghee to reduce saturated fat intake, and monitor overall sodium intake across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from animal-derived butter promotes pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production; sodium at 632mg/100g exacerbates tissue oedema associated with RA flares.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit banana prata, request vegetable oil substitution for butter, and minimise added honey or sugar toppings that promote systemic inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana prata is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a flaky, layered wheat-flour flatbread (roti prata) pan-fried in butter or ghee and filled or topped with sliced or mashed banana. It is a variant of the classic roti prata, which traces its origins to South Indian and Malay culinary traditions and is now a staple of Singapore's multicultural food culture. The dough is made from wheat flour, water, egg, salt, and fat (butter or ghee), then stretched thin, folded repeatedly to create layers, and cooked on a hot griddle until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The banana filling adds natural sweetness and a soft, slightly caramelised texture. Common serving variants include banana butter prata (with added butter) and banana honey prata (drizzled with honey). Nutritionally, banana prata is energy-dense at approximately 412 kcal per 100g, with a moderate protein content of 9.85g, relatively low total fat at 8.25g, and a high carbohydrate load of 75.2g per 100g. Dietary fibre is notably high at 10.2g per 100g, which partially moderates the glycaemic response. Sodium is elevated at 632mg per 100g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.5g per 100g from butter and ghee, relevant for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal and vegetarian but contains gluten, egg, and dairy allergens. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a late-night supper at Indian Muslim prata shops and hawker centres across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in banana prata?
Is banana prata high in sodium?
How much carbohydrate in banana prata?
Is banana prata suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of banana prata?
How much does one piece of banana prata weigh?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (paratha cooked with ghee/butter) as the closest approved-source analogue and documented as an adapted estimate. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source food (roti/chapati, University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and documented as an analogous estimate. No forbidden sources are used.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for banana prata is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g set to -1: trans fat content for banana prata is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation as of 2026; the input record cited GrabFood (a forbidden source) for this value, which has been removed. 3. vitamin_c_mg set to -1: vitamin C content for the composite banana prata dish is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; the input record cited GrabFood (a forbidden source) for this value, which has been removed. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for banana prata and are not available from USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 5. saturated_fat_g (3.5) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central (paratha, whole wheat, cooked with ghee/butter, FDC ID 2346413); the HPB Foodo entry does not report saturated fat separately. The input record cited GrabFood (5.0g/100g) for this value, which has been replaced with the USDA-based estimate. 6. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry. 7. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on roti/chapati (wheat flour flatbread) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for banana prata exists in the approved GI source database. 8. Two portions are listed as is_default: true in the input (180g and 200g); the 180g portion (banana butter prata) is used as the canonical serving size for GL calculation. 9. The input description references honey as an ingredient; honey is not universally present in all banana prata preparations — it is specific to the banana honey prata variant.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed GrabFood as a data source entirely — GrabFood is a forbidden source under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md source policy.

Replaced saturated_fat_g value (previously 5.0 from GrabFood) with 3.5g/100g estimated from USDA FoodData Central (paratha, whole wheat, cooked with ghee/butter, FDC ID 2346413) as the closest approved-source analogue.
Set trans_fat_g to -1 (data not available in approved sources) replacing the GrabFood-sourced value of 0.25.
Set vitamin_c_mg to -1 (data not available in approved sources) replacing the GrabFood-sourced value of 2.5.
Updated nutrient_source to reference only HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources.
Updated gi_source to explicitly cite Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) alongside the University of Sydney GI Database to strengthen GI source compliance.
Updated notes to document all -1 fields including the newly set trans_fat_g and vitamin_c_mg, with explicit reference to the removal of GrabFood data.
Updated health_context cardiovascular_disease key_factor to reflect revised saturated_fat_g estimate of 3.5g/100g.
